plan:
- Microcontroller: Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W or ESP32-CAM with integrated Wi-Fi for image capture and transmission. Select a compatible camera module (e.g., Raspberry Pi Camera Module V2).
- AI Processing: Implement on-device inference for compostable identification using a pre-trained TinyML model or an optional AI accelerator like a Google Coral USB Accelerator if using a Raspberry Pi.
- Power System: Rechargeable LiPo battery pack (e.g., 18650 cells with protection circuit) providing sufficient run-time, potentially integrated with a small solar panel for extended life if lighting permits.
- Enclosure & Mounting: IP67-rated waterproof and tamper-resistant enclosure to protect electronics from elements and trashcan environment. Design custom mounting brackets or clamps for secure attachment inside the trash can.
- Mechanical Components: Stainless steel fasteners, rubber gaskets for waterproofing, and an impact-resistant clear polycarbonate window for the camera lens.
- Custom 3D-Printed Parts: Internal component mounts, camera lens shroud, custom enclosure seals, and potentially specialized mounting adapters for trash can geometry.

nodes.name: Main Controller
nodes.description: Main microcontroller for image processing (ML) and motor control.

nodes.name: Camera Sensor
nodes.description: RPI CAM V2 IMX219 8MP

nodes.name: Cellular Data Modem
nodes.description: Low-power cellular module for remote location data transmission, providing real-time live tracking capabilities.

nodes.name: Battery Management System
nodes.productName: TP4056 Li-Ion Charger Module with Protection

nodes.name: Voltage Regulator for Pi

examples.title: Basic MP1584EN DC-DC Buck Converter Module Tutorial
examples.description: This tutorial provides a fundamental guide on how to use the MP1584EN buck converter module, covering its input/output connections, voltage adjustment using the onboard potentiometer, and basic testing procedures. It's a great starting point for understanding its operation before integration into more complex projects.

examples.title: Powering ESP32 and Sensors with MP1584EN
examples.description: While not exclusively about MP1584EN, this guide details power supply considerations for ESP32 and related sensors, often recommending buck converters like the MP1584EN to step down higher voltages (e.g., 12V) to the required 5V or 3.3V for microcontrollers and peripherals. It demonstrates practical application in a common MCU project scenario.

examples.title: Making a Simple Bench Power Supply with MP1584EN
examples.description: This Instructable demonstrates building a compact, adjustable bench power supply using the MP1584EN module. It showcases the converter's ability to take a higher input voltage (e.g., from a laptop charger) and provide a regulated, adjustable output for prototyping and testing various electronic circuits, illustrating its versatility.

examples.title: MP1584EN Buck Converter for Raspberry Pi Power Supply
examples.description: This forum discussion and related resources illustrate how the MP1584EN is used to convert a 12V input, typically from a car battery or a 12V power adapter, down to the stable 5V required by a Raspberry Pi. It highlights practical considerations for ensuring sufficient current delivery and stable voltage for SBCs.

examples.title: Automotive 12V to 5V USB Charger using MP1584EN
examples.description: This project details how to construct a simple 12V to 5V USB charger for automotive use employing the MP1584EN. It demonstrates a common application where the converter efficiently steps down the vehicle's 12V supply to charge USB-powered devices, providing a robust solution for mobile power needs.

nodes.description: Regulates power from the main 12V supply to 5V for ESP32-S3 modules and various sensors.

originalPrompt: I want to build a small camera that can sit inside of a nyc public trashcans and what it needs to be able to do is to take photos if the trash and identify what is compostable.

subSteps.title: 3D print all custom mechanical components
Insert M3 threaded inserts into main enclosure and internal base plate
Test fit the clear polycarbonate window and enclosure gasket into the main enclosure
Test fit electrical components into their respective 3D printed mounts
Connect battery pack to battery management system
Wire battery management system output to buck converter input
Connect buck converter output to main MCU and cellular modem power inputs
Connect camera module to main MCU for power and CSI-2 data
Wire cellular modem to main MCU for UART data and GPIO control
Connect AI accelerator to main MCU via USB 3.0 Type-C for power and data
Perform continuity and polarity checks on all power connections
Apply initial power to the main MCU and verify power indicator LEDs
Flash base firmware onto the main MCU
Test camera module detection and acquire a test image
Verify cellular modem communication via UART and test basic AT commands
Confirm AI accelerator detection and run a diagnostic test
Monitor battery charging/discharging with the BMS
Mount the camera module into its mount and attach to the internal base plate
Mount main MCU, cellular modem, battery pack, BMS, buck converter, and AI accelerator to their respective mounts and then to the internal base plate
Install the camera lens shroud and secure the clear polycarbonate window with the gasket into the main enclosure
Mount the assembled internal base plate with all components into the main enclosure
Secure the trashcan clamp top to the main enclosure
Assemble the trashcan clamp bottom with the M5 thumb screw and nut
Attach cable management clips to internal base plate and route wires neatly
Close the main enclosure lid and secure with M3 screws

projectDescription: A small, battery-powered camera system, equipped with a Main Controller and Camera Sensor, integrates into NYC public trashcans to identify compostable waste using AI, transmitting data via a Cellular Data Modem. Encased in a durable enclosure, it utilizes clamps for secure internal mounting.

imagePromptSnapshot.description:
Project idea: I want to build a small camera that can sit inside of a nyc public trashcans and what it needs to be able to do is to take photos if the trash and identify what is compostable.

Design notes: small camera, sit inside, NYC trashcans, identify compostable

Electrical components (current): Main Controller (mcu, 65x30x5mm); Camera Sensor (sensor, 25x23x9mm); 2x module: Cellular Data Modem, AI Accelerator (Optional); 3x power: Li-Ion Battery Pack, Battery Management System, Voltage Regulator for Pi

Mechanical/structural parts (current): 2x enclosure: Main Enclosure, Camera Lens Window; 6x misc: Enclosure Gasket, Enclosure Lid Screws, Internal Component Mount Screws, M3 Heat-Set Inserts +2 more; 12x 3d_printed: Camera Module Mount, MCU Mount Plate, Cellular Modem Mount, Battery Pack Holder +8 more

Assembly: Main Enclosure → Enclosure Gasket (seals against); Main Enclosure → Camera Lens Window (sealed into opening with gasket); Main Enclosure → Enclosure Lid Screws (accepts M3 screws for lid); Main Enclosure → Internal Base Plate (mounted inside with M2 screws); M3 Heat-Set Inserts → Main Enclosure (inserted into posts/bosses); M3 Heat-Set Inserts → Internal Base Plate (inserted into posts/bosses); Camera Module Mount → Internal Base Plate (mounted with M2 screws); MCU Mount Plate → Internal Base Plate (mounted with M2 screws); Cellular Modem Mount → Internal Base Plate (mounted with M2 screws); Battery Pack Holder → Internal Base Plate (mounted with M2 screws); Battery Management Mount → Internal Base Plate (mounted with M2 screws); Buck Converter Mount → Internal Base Plate (mounted with M2 screws); AI Accelerator Mount → Internal Base Plate (mounted with M2 screws); Internal Component Mount Screws → Internal Base Plate (threaded into); Camera Lens Shroud → Camera Lens Window (press-fit around camera side) (+12 more connections)

assumptions: Basic soldering experience with small components
Familiarity with 3D printer operation and material handling
Basic electronics knowledge for power and data connections
Development environment set up for main_mcu (e.g., OS, SDK, IDE)
Ability to flash firmware onto main_mcu
